content:
Hand-crafted predictive attributes predate modern AutoML. Statisticians in **survey and econometrics** routinely constructed **derived variables**—income per household member, age squared for nonlinear effects—because linear models demanded explicit structure.

**Expert systems and scorecards** in banking and insurance (1980s–2000s) encoded domain transforms as auditable rules: debt-to-income ratios, tenure flags, and calendar seasonality baked into logistic scorecards.

**Kaggle-era tabular competitions (2010s)** popularized aggressive feature engineering—count encoding, groupby aggregates, datetime decompositions—showing that thoughtful creation often beat raw columns with gradient boosting.

**Automated feature tools** (Featuretools, AutoFeat) and deep tabular models reduce manual work, but production teams still rely on **explicit domain features** for interpretability, compliance, and training-serving parity. scikit-learn's **FunctionTransformer** and **ColumnTransformer** integrate hand-defined transforms into Pipelines alongside learned extractors.

timeline.label: Derived variables in statistics
timeline.description: Econometric models use constructed ratios and polynomials because OLS cannot invent nonlinear structure on its own.

timeline.label: Credit scorecards
timeline.description: Financial institutions codify domain transforms as transparent features for regulated decision systems.

timeline.label: Kaggle feature engineering
timeline.description: Groupby aggregates and datetime tricks become standard practice for tree models on heterogeneous tabular data.

timeline.label: Automated feature synthesis
timeline.description: Libraries propose candidate features from relational schemas; human domain columns remain for semantics and governance.

timeline.label: Pipeline-native transforms
timeline.description: sklearn FunctionTransformer and pandas-first workflows keep creation logic versioned next to encoders and estimators.
